title: Finding Better AI Opportunities
summary: Identify where AI creates real value by examining work patterns, friction points, and decision-heavy tasks with a clear evaluation lens.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Adopt The Opportunity Audit Mindset
lessons.subtitle: Look for repeated work, information friction, and review-heavy bottlenecks.
lessons.objective: Find AI opportunities by observing work patterns instead of chasing trends.
lessons.useWhen: Look for repeated work, information friction, and review-heavy bottlenecks.
lessons.whyItMatters: The best AI opportunities usually come from boring recurring pain, not from abstract innovation sessions.

coreConcepts.title: Look For Repetition
coreConcepts.text: Repeated drafting, summarizing, organizing, and comparison work are common signals.

coreConcepts.title: Look For Delay
coreConcepts.text: Waiting for documentation, follow-up, or synthesis often points to high-value use cases.

coreConcepts.title: Look For Review Bottlenecks
coreConcepts.text: If experts spend time reformatting or clarifying work, AI may help reduce that burden.

workedExample.scenario: A business operations team feels busy but cannot name where AI would help.
workedExample.approach: They review a week of work and highlight repeated drafting, recap, and coordination tasks.
workedExample.result: Several realistic AI use cases appear quickly.
workedExample.verification: They confirm the tasks are truly recurring rather than one-off spikes.

doThisNow.task: Review the last week of your work and list three recurring tasks that match the audit lens.
steps: Scan your calendar or task list.
Choose the one with the clearest pain.

commonMistakes: Starting from tool ideas instead of work pain.
Choosing flashy use cases over recurring ones.

roleVariants.manager: Model safe adoption through small pilots, clear examples, and lightweight team habits.
roleVariants.writer: Document the prompts, examples, and edits that help others replicate quality.
roleVariants.analyst: Define simple measures and evidence standards so adoption conversations stay grounded.
roleVariants.operator: Focus on workflow change and training, not just tool access.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Classify Tasks: Automate, Augment, Avoid
lessons.subtitle: Not every task should be given to AI, and not every good use case deserves full automation.
lessons.objective: Sort tasks into the right level of AI support.
lessons.useWhen: Not every task should be given to AI, and not every good use case deserves full automation.
lessons.whyItMatters: The right level of intervention depends on risk, ambiguity, and how much judgment the task requires.

coreConcepts.title: Automate Bounded Repetition
coreConcepts.text: Low-risk, repeatable sequences with clear review paths are the best automation candidates.

coreConcepts.title: Augment Judgment-Heavy Work
coreConcepts.text: Many tasks benefit most from AI support rather than full handoff.

coreConcepts.title: Avoid High-Risk, Low-Clarity Tasks
coreConcepts.text: If the task is sensitive, ambiguous, or hard to review, AI may not be the right first move.

workedExample.scenario: A team debates whether to automate customer escalations.
workedExample.approach: They classify the work and realize drafting summaries is augmentable, but outbound decisions should not be automated.
workedExample.result: The scope becomes safer and more realistic.
workedExample.verification: They review whether the classification reflects the actual consequences of being wrong.

artifact.label: Classifier
artifact.title: Task classification questions
items: How much judgment does it require?
How easy is it to review?

doThisNow.task: Classify three current tasks as automate, augment, or avoid.
steps: Assign the level of AI support.

commonMistakes: Trying to automate judgment-heavy work first.
Treating every good use case as an automation candidate.

roleVariants.manager: Model safe adoption through small pilots, clear examples, and lightweight team habits.
roleVariants.writer: Document the prompts, examples, and edits that help others replicate quality.
roleVariants.analyst: Define simple measures and evidence standards so adoption conversations stay grounded.
roleVariants.operator: Focus on workflow change and training, not just tool access.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Estimate Value Quickly
lessons.subtitle: Rough ROI thinking is enough to prioritize early opportunities.
lessons.objective: Use simple value estimates to compare candidate AI opportunities.
lessons.useWhen: Rough ROI thinking is enough to prioritize early opportunities.
lessons.whyItMatters: You do not need perfect ROI math to decide what to test next, but you do need a way to compare options.

coreConcepts.title: Estimate Frequency
coreConcepts.text: How often does the work happen?

coreConcepts.title: Estimate Current Effort
coreConcepts.text: How much time, rework, or coordination does it currently require?

coreConcepts.title: Estimate Adoption Cost
coreConcepts.text: Training, setup, risk controls, and change effort all count.

workedExample.scenario: A team must choose between automating weekly status updates or improving vendor research.
workedExample.approach: They compare frequency, current effort, and change cost.
workedExample.result: The priority becomes clearer without elaborate spreadsheets.
workedExample.verification: They sanity-check the estimates with the people doing the work each week.

doThisNow.task: Score two candidate AI opportunities with rough ROI estimates.
steps: Estimate the change cost and likely gain.

roleVariants.manager: Model safe adoption through small pilots, clear examples, and lightweight team habits.
roleVariants.writer: Document the prompts, examples, and edits that help others replicate quality.
roleVariants.analyst: Define simple measures and evidence standards so adoption conversations stay grounded.
roleVariants.operator: Focus on workflow change and training, not just tool access.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Start With Quick Wins
lessons.subtitle: The first AI opportunity should teach you something and lower adoption friction.
lessons.objective: Choose first opportunities that are visible, safe, and teachable.
lessons.useWhen: The first AI opportunity should teach you something and lower adoption friction.
lessons.whyItMatters: Quick wins create evidence and confidence. They also reveal what support structures you actually need.

coreConcepts.title: Pick Visible Value
coreConcepts.text: The improvement should be easy for others to notice and understand.

coreConcepts.title: Keep The Workflow Narrow
coreConcepts.text: A clear boundary reduces implementation and review complexity.

coreConcepts.title: Choose Teachable Work
coreConcepts.text: The best first wins are easy to explain and repeat.

workedExample.scenario: A team considers a complex workflow automation as its first AI initiative.
workedExample.approach: They instead start with a repeatable meeting follow-up workflow that saves time and is easy to review.
workedExample.result: They get a cleaner early win and learn faster.
workedExample.verification: They compare the before-and-after workflow to confirm the gain is real and visible.

doThisNow.task: Choose the best first AI opportunity from your current shortlist.
steps: Write why it is the right first move.

commonMistakes: Picking the most ambitious use case first.
Choosing invisible back-office wins nobody notices.
Ignoring whether others can learn the workflow.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Write An Opportunity Brief
lessons.subtitle: A short brief is enough to align people on what you want to test.
lessons.objective: Draft a one-page brief for an AI opportunity you want to pursue.
lessons.useWhen: A short brief is enough to align people on what you want to test.
lessons.whyItMatters: A brief turns a good idea into something others can evaluate, support, or refine.

coreConcepts.title: Describe The Current Pain
coreConcepts.text: State what the workflow looks like today and why it is worth improving.

coreConcepts.title: Describe The Proposed Change
coreConcepts.text: Show exactly where AI would help and where people still review.

coreConcepts.title: Describe Success
coreConcepts.text: Define the outcome, not just the implementation.

workedExample.scenario: An individual contributor wants approval to test AI-assisted research summaries.
workedExample.approach: They write a short brief with current effort, proposed workflow, review rules, and simple success measures.
workedExample.result: The idea becomes easy to discuss and approve.
workedExample.verification: They review the brief to ensure it describes the real workflow instead of a vague AI ambition.

doThisNow.task: Write an opportunity brief for one AI use case you want to test.
steps: State how you will know if it works.

commonMistakes: Writing about AI in general instead of one workflow.

outcomes: Find high-value AI opportunities in real work.
Sort tasks into automate, augment, or avoid.
nextModuleBridge: Next, support those opportunities with clear ownership, change habits, and governance.
